---
title: "Economists under Constraint: Controversies and the Construction of Climate Finance as an Economic Object (1990–2025)"
author: "[Anonymous]"
keywords:
  - climate finance
  - quantification
  - accounting categories
  - international organisations
jel:
  - B20
  - Q54
  - F35
  - Q56
date: "2026-07-08"
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
metadata-files: [manuscript-vars.yml]
bibliography: bibliography/main.bib
csl: bibliography/oeconomia.csl
number-sections: true
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          \usepackage{etoolbox}
          \renewcommand{\CSLBlock}[1]{\newline\noindent #1}
---

<!-- Phase 2→3 contract: this manuscript consumes
     - figures/fig_bars_v1.png          (@fig-bars)
     - figures/fig_breaks.png           (@fig-breaks)
     - figures/fig_composition.png      (@fig-composition)
     - tables/tab_venues.md             (@tbl-venues)
     Any change to these outputs requires rebuilding the manuscript.

     English translation of the frozen French source content/manuscript-Gide.qmd
     (tag gide-submitted-2026-06-29), machine-performed and style-anchored on the
     author's pre-AI English prose (docs/style-anchor-v205.md, ticket 0173). -->

**Abstract.** Since the early 1990s, climate finance has moved from a vague expression of international policy to a central economic object, quantified, compared and audited in the climate negotiations, at the OECD and in the multilateral development banks. Successive collective commitments -- from the goal of $100 billion per year adopted at Copenhagen in 2009 to the new quantified goal of $300 billion by 2035 set at Baku in 2024 -- have made climate finance a nodal point of international climate governance. Yet after three decades of statistical effort, controversies persist over what should be counted as climate finance, how to value it, and how to attribute responsibilities among actors.

We propose a historical analysis of the construction of climate finance as an economic object between 1990 and 2025. It departs from a reading that would make climate finance a mere technical extension of environmental economics, or of a climate economics grounded in integrated modelling. The founding work on externalities and collective environmental constraints [@ayres_kneese1969], then the models centred on growth, damages, risk and time [@nordhaus1992; @manne_richels1992; @stern2007; @weitzman2007], structured part of climate expertise; the question of sharing the international financial effort, however, developed largely outside these frames.

Climate finance emerged mainly through the entanglement of international negotiations, development finance practices, and the accounting tools mobilised to make political commitments operational. At the OECD, and within the Development Assistance Committee in particular, economists such as Jan Corfee-Morlot played a central role in building statistical infrastructures -- Rio markers, grant-equivalent valuation methods, monitoring and reporting frameworks -- which made climate finance visible and measurable within the existing frames of official development assistance [@corfee-morlot2009; @corfee-morlot2012].

These accounting categories soon became sites of controversy within the economics profession. On one side, approaches grounded in the correction of market failures emphasised efficiency, leverage and the mobilisation of private capital, justifying blended finance and de-risking instruments. On the other, economists working from a political-economy perspective contested both the measurement conventions and their normative implications. Axel and Katharina Michaelowa exposed the incentives to over-report inherent in the Rio markers [@michaelowa2007], while Romain Weikmans and J. Timmons Roberts read the recurring controversies as the expression of unresolved distributive conflicts between North and South [@roberts_weikmans2017; @weikmans_roberts2019].

Through four recurring controversies -- the valuation of concessional loans, the credibility of the Rio markers, the attribution of mobilised private finance, and the boundary between development aid and climate obligations -- we show that economists acted as technical experts and, beyond that, as actors at the science-society boundary, permanently redefining what climate finance is and ought to be. Climate finance thus stands out as a privileged case for analysing the political limits of economic quantification in international governance.

**Keywords:** climate finance, quantification, accounting categories, international organisations

**JEL codes:** B20, Q54, F35, Q56

\newpage

## Introduction: a precise number, an unstable object {#sec-intro}

In November 2024, in Baku, climate negotiators adopted a new collective goal: developed countries should mobilise $300 billion per year by 2035 for climate action in developing countries. The number is sharp. The object it designates is much less so. No fund holds these 300 billion. No single ledger records them. No counting method commands assent. The number is produced, like the $100 billion per year announced at Copenhagen in 2009, by applying disputed accounting conventions to heterogeneous financial flows: grants, concessional loans, non-concessional loans, guarantees, equity stakes, private co-financing, multilateral contributions.

This paradox is what makes climate finance an interesting object for the history of economic thought. Climate finance is treated today as a self-evident economic quantity: it is tracked, compared, audited and commented upon in reports from the OECD, the UNFCCC, the multilateral development banks and NGOs. Yet its perimeter remains disputed. Should a loan be counted at face value, or only at its grant equivalent? Can a public guarantee bring the private investment it accompanies into the climate finance total? Does an ordinary development project become climatic because the institution that finances it marks it as such? These questions are key to determining whether a country honours its international commitments, to separating development aid from external debt, and to gauging how new and additional the international effort of climate solidarity really is.

They also put a more general question to the history of economic thought: how can a quantity whose perimeter remains contested become a legitimate economic variable? Climate finance forces us to take seriously quantities that function before being fully defined. It is neither a pure accounting illusion nor a natural variable waiting to be measured. It is an operative category, stabilised by conventions, institutions and reporting practices, yet permanently exposed to controversy.

The thesis defended here is simple: economists did not discover climate finance the way one discovers a reality already there. The financial flows themselves exist: public budgets, loans, guarantees, equity stakes and private investment. What is constructed is their aggregation into a governable quantity. Economists contributed to this construction by devising the accounting categories, the concessionality thresholds, the purpose markers and the attribution methods that make otherwise heterogeneous flows comparable. This work was not carried out in academic journals alone. It played out in international organisations, statistical committees, expert reports, development banks and reporting procedures. The economics at stake here is an economics of measurement conventions, more than one of equilibrium models or cost-benefit calculation.

The phrase in the title -- "economists under constraint" -- is to be taken literally. The constraint is diplomatic first: the amounts are fixed before the object is stabilised. It is also statistical, because the apparatus of official development assistance pre-exists and imposes its categories. It is political, finally: the same instruments must reconcile two poorly compatible requirements, the efficiency of mobilisation and the accountability of transfers. Economists act neither as sovereigns nor as mere executants; they work in this constrained space, where every measurement convention is also a compromise.

The literature on the history of climate economics has mostly studied modelling: intertemporal optimisation, the choice of the discount rate, the value of carbon, integrated assessment models [@pottier2016; @aykut_dahan2015]. These works are essential for understanding how climate change was constituted as an economic problem. They say less about how climate-related financial flows were constituted as a measurable object. The shift matters. Moving from the question "what should the price of carbon be?" to the question "does this dollar count as climate finance?" amounts to changing intellectual regime: from the model to accounting, and from optimisation to commensuration [@espeland_stevens1998; @merry2016]. It also means changing the type of economic rationality: no longer only computing an optimal trajectory, but producing a quantity solid enough to verify political commitments, and supple enough to remain negotiable.

This text orders economic thought on climate finance into three eras. During the first, up to the mid-2000s, several traditions exist separately: environmental economics, development economics, the economics of international effort-sharing. None of them produces climate finance on its own. The second period, 2007--2014, is one of crystallization: Bali and Copenhagen create the demand for measurement, the OECD/DAC and the multilateral banks supply the counting infrastructures. The third era, since 2015, is that of controversies around the existing object. A concrete operation -- a World Bank financing in Türkiye -- serves to show how these controversies become operative. The conclusion returns to the main result: climate finance became governable through the ambiguity of its categories, not despite it.

## Method

This text proposes a history of economic thought outside its canonical sites. It follows the models, the theoretical articles and the academic controversies less than the operative categories through which economists in international organisations, development banks and expert networks made a diplomatic commitment calculable. Climate finance thus appears as a borderline object: neither a simple application of environmental economics nor a simple extension of development aid, but the product of a practical economics of measurement.

The proposed periodization follows from our reading of the texts, from participant observation of the debates, notably on integrated modelling and at the IPCC, and from the analysis of the climate negotiations. To test it, we add a computational analysis of a multilingual corpus on climate finance (@fig-bars). The point is to submit the interpretation to an independent test, short of a falsification attempt under established disciplinary criteria; computation does not replace interpretation here. A quantitative method seemed useful to us to illustrate the argument, at the border between the history of economic thought and science-and-society studies.

This way of doing intellectual history may surprise. The discipline usually reads a small number of canonical texts closely. Yet climate finance has no canon. At least, if a canon exists in the field, our harvest of the climate finance course syllabi findable on the internet did not bring it up. Climate finance took shape recently, in thousands of expert reports, institutional decisions and scattered articles, in several languages -- a literature too vast and too fragmented for a single reader to hold entire. We build an empirical corpus that aims at exhaustiveness, in which institutional publications take an important place. This matches the nature of the object under study: climate finance is not defined by a single theory published in a small set of Anglo-American academic outputs.

![Growth of the climate finance literature, 1990--2023. The field is nearly nonexistent before 2007, structures itself after Bali and Copenhagen, then develops within the categories stabilised after Paris.](figures/fig_bars_v1.png){#fig-bars width=85%}

The historical argument covers the period 1990--2025. The systematic bibliographic corpus stops at 2024, the last complete year available at the time of the analysis; the year 2025 enters through the Türkiye case, as a contemporary test of the stabilised categories.

The computational analysis of about {{< meta corpus_total_approx >}} works is discussed in the appendix. The results provide convergent clues: the lexical breaks, the co-citation communities and the thematic recomposition of the corpus are consistent with a periodization in three moments: before climate finance (1990--2006), crystallization (2007--2014), an established and contested field (2015--2024). Thus @fig-breaks shows the speed at which the themes treated in the literature renew themselves. The index is expressed in relative units and computed with no prior knowledge of the periodization. The results support our reading in two breaks. The lexicographic shifts tabulated in the appendix show, between periods, the retreat of terms tied to the Clean Development Mechanism and the Kyoto vocabulary, and the advance of terms tied to mobilised finance, funds, flows, aid and, later, green finance.

![Speed of thematic renewal in the climate finance literature: the field went through two spells of faster change. See the qualitative recomposition of the themes treated in appendix A.5.](figures/fig_breaks.png){#fig-breaks width=85%}

## Before climate finance: three disjoint traditions (1990--2006) {#sec-before}

The United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, adopted in 1992, does not yet speak of "climate finance". Its Article 4.3 states a much more precise obligation: developed countries shall provide new and additional financial resources to cover the agreed costs, in particular the "agreed full incremental costs" of the measures implemented by developing countries [@unfccc1992]. The logic is transactional: defined and accepted costs are to be reimbursed; no worldwide aggregate of financial flows is to be compiled. The later history can be read as the gradual abandonment of this initial precision in favour of a broader, handier and more ambiguous category: climate finance.

This section shows why none of the three languages available before 2007 -- carbon price, development aid, effort-sharing -- sufficed to produce an accounting of climate finance. Before the Copenhagen COP, three intellectual traditions each address one part of the problem.

The first is environmental economics. From Pigou onward, then with the work on externalities and material constraints [@ayres_kneese1969], it treats pollution as a divergence between private and social costs. In the climate case, this tradition produces one dominant question: what price should emissions carry? The integrated models developed by Nordhaus, Manne and Richels, then discussed by Stern and Weitzman, compute optimal abatement paths, future damages, discount rates and implicit carbon prices [@nordhaus1992; @manne_richels1992; @stern2007; @weitzman2007]. They powerfully structure the economics of climate change. But they do not build a category of climate finance. The North-South flows that may appear in these models are optimisation or welfare variables, not institutional instruments to be tracked and verified.

The second tradition is development economics and, more precisely, aid statistics. The OECD's Development Assistance Committee has tracked official development assistance since the 1960s: grants, loans, bilateral and multilateral channels, sectors, purposes. There is nothing natural about these categories, but the effort of transparency is useful in a world where questions of aid, trade, influence and public integrity intertwine. They are the outcome of decades of statistical negotiation, stabilised in directives and made progressively objective through use [@desrosieres1998; @porter1995]. The notions of concessionality, face value and grant equivalent belong first to this universe. The Rio markers, introduced in 1998 to identify projects linked to the conventions issued from the Rio Summit, then provide a decisive bridge: they make it possible to affix a climate label to existing aid activities. Climate finance would thus inherit a statistical infrastructure designed for development aid, with its strengths, its biases and its reporting incentives.

The third tradition is that of international effort-sharing. The climate negotiations of the 1990s are steeped in the principles of common but differentiated responsibility, equity, historical emissions and respective capabilities. In economic modelling, these questions cross the literature on welfare weights and cost allocation [@negishi1960]. In the negotiations, they take a legal and political form: who must pay? Yet this tradition remains largely disconnected from the operational question: how to count what has actually been paid? The Kyoto Protocol and the Clean Development Mechanism do bring financial flows and climate objectives closer, but through carbon credits and market mechanisms, not through an accounting of climate finance in dollars [@michaelowa2019].

We tested this dispersion in the corpus by examining the references used in writings prior to 2007. Modellers of climate policy economics, aid specialists and scholars of international governance turn out to share few central references. This fragmentation matches the intellectual state of the problem. Before 2007 the pieces exist, but the object is not assembled. Environmental economics produces the reasoning of efficiency and the carbon price. Development economics supplies the categories and registers of aid. The effort-sharing debates supply the grammar of responsibility. Climate finance appears only when a quantified political commitment forces these three ensembles to work together.

| Tradition | Central question | Authors and institutions | Contribution to climate finance | Limit before 2007 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Environmental economics | What price for carbon? | Ayres, Kneese; Nordhaus; Manne and Richels; Stern; Weitzman | Externalities, cost-benefit analysis, implicit carbon price, abatement efficiency | No accounting of North-South flows |
| Development economics | How to classify and count aid? | OECD/DAC; statistical directives; Rio markers; Michaelowa | ODA, concessionality, grant equivalent, bilateral and multilateral channels | Categories designed for aid, not for climate obligations |
| International effort-sharing | Who must pay? | UNFCCC; Kyoto; Negishi; debates on equity and differentiated responsibilities | Transfer principle, incremental costs, North-South responsibilities | Weak translation into instruments of financial tracking |

: Three traditions before climate finance {#tbl-traditions}

## Crystallization: Copenhagen and the making of the accounts (2007--2014) {#sec-crystallization}

The moment of crystallization proceeded from a political constraint, not from a theoretical discovery. The Bali Action Plan, in 2007, requires that financial commitments be measurable, reportable and verifiable [@unfccc2007bali]. Two years later, at Copenhagen, developed countries commit to mobilising $100 billion per year by 2020. The figure is not derived from an economic model or from an estimate of needs. It results from a negotiation. Once announced, however, it creates an obligation to measure. To know whether 100 billion have been mobilised, one must say what counts.

It is in this limited sense that the Copenhagen commitment is performative [@callon1998]. It does not create the financial flows themselves: loans, grants, guarantees and investments existed already. It does oblige institutions to construct the aggregate it promises to measure. Performativity does not pass here through a market model, as in MacKenzie's analyses of financial formulas [@mackenzie2006]. It passes through forms, statistical directives, databases, coefficients and attribution conventions. Climate finance becomes a governable quantity because it becomes countable.

The OECD/DAC then plays a decisive role. The practical choice is to graft climate onto the existing apparatus of official development assistance. The Rio markers let donors declare whether an activity targets climate as its principal objective, as a significant objective, or not at all. This solution is efficient: it avoids creating an entirely new reporting system. But it carries the conventions and biases of ODA over into climate finance. Donors declare the markers themselves. Projects with a "significant" objective can be counted with varying coefficients. Loans can be recorded at face value or at grant equivalent. A device designed as a monitoring tool thus becomes the frame of a politically charged international accounting.

Economists in international organisations occupy a specific position in this period. They are neither mere technicians nor the sovereign authors of an architecture. They answer a demand issued from the negotiations, the states and the NGOs: make the commitments verifiable with the statistical instruments at hand. But by making the flows comparable, they also define what becomes visible. The work of Jan Corfee-Morlot and her colleagues at the OECD, that of the Climate Policy Initiative, and the reports of the multilateral banks do more than observe climate finance; they fix its operative categories [@corfee-morlot2009; @corfee-morlot2012; @buchner2011; @buchner2013]. It is a typical case of economisation: a domain becomes economic because it is equipped with calculation devices [@caliskan_callon2009; @caliskan_callon2010].

Two vocabularies stabilise then, and with them two durable poles of the field. The first is an efficiency pole. It speaks of leverage, blended finance, de-risking, private capital mobilisation and the correction of market failures. In this perspective, public climate finance must be catalytic: it serves to alter the risk-return profile of projects so as to attract private capital. The second is an accountability pole. It speaks of additionality, double counting, grant equivalent, climate justice, aid quality. In this perspective, climate finance is first of all a commitment of the developed countries towards the developing countries, and the main problem is the donors' tendency to relabel existing flows.

| Pole | Dominant question | Preferred concepts | Typical institutions and sites | Political risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | How to raise investment volumes? | Leverage, blended finance, de-risking, private mobilisation, green bonds | Multilateral banks, OECD, sustainable finance, energy economics | Diluting the climate obligation in financial engineering |
| Accountability | How to verify that the transfer is real? | Additionality, grant equivalent, double counting, Rio markers, climate justice | NGOs, UNFCCC, political economy, critical reports | Reducing climate finance to an insoluble distributive dispute |

: Two constitutive poles of the field {#tbl-poles}

These two poles are not external to each other. They use the same categories but interpret them differently. For the efficiency pole, a development loan can be a useful resource if it unlocks a low-carbon investment. For the accountability pole, the same loan can be additional debt that should only be counted up to its grant element. For the former, the ambiguity of the categories eases action; for the latter, it makes the inflation of the numbers possible. Controversy is therefore not an accident of climate finance. It is one of its operating conditions.

## Counting is governing: controversies in an established field (2015--2025) {#sec-controverses}

The Paris Agreement gives climate finance a central legal status. Article 9 provides that developed countries shall provide financial resources to assist developing countries with both mitigation and adaptation, and that this mobilisation shall progress beyond previous efforts [@unfccc2015paris]. The contrast with 1992 is striking. Where the Convention spoke of agreed incremental costs, Paris speaks simply of financial resources and mobilisation. The term is broader but less precise. The category is settled enough to be used as if it went without saying, yet it remains indeterminate enough to keep sustaining divergent interpretations.

### One operation, several ways of counting: the Türkiye / World Bank case {#sec-transaction}

A single operation is enough to lay bare the mechanics of these controversies. In 2025, the World Bank approves financing for the Turkish project *Transforming Power Transmission System* (TPTS, P508354), meant to modernise the power grid and allow the direct integration of 1.7 gigawatts of renewable energy.^[World Bank, "World Bank and Clean Technology Fund Support Türkiye's Renewable Energy Goals with Power Transmission Project", press release of 4 August 2025, project *Transforming Power Transmission System* (P508354). Amounts confirmed by the press release and by the project information document (World Bank, PID PIDIA01318, 28 April 2025): IBRD loan of €625 million, or $707.9 million equivalent; CTF loan of €32.8 million, or $38 million; CTF grant of $2 million.] The operation combines three instruments: an IBRD loan of 625 million euros at about 3%, a concessional loan from the Clean Technology Fund of 32.8 million euros, about 38 million dollars, with a 0.25% service fee, and a grant of 2 million dollars.^[The estimate of the full cost of the IBRD loan combines the three-month Euribor, around 2.1% in mid-2025, and the Bank's standard margin. The rate and maturity conditions of the CTF loan are not detailed in the public project information document and fall under the standard concessional scale of the Climate Investment Funds; the assumption retained here -- a rate of about 0.25%, a maturity of fifteen to twenty years for a middle-income country -- is indicative. The Turkish sovereign rate quoted reflects market conditions in mid-2025, the country's rating having been upgraded in 2024-2025 while remaining below investment grade.] At the same moment Türkiye, rated below investment grade, borrows dollars on the markets at about 7.2--7.75%.

One clarification averts a misreading. The multilateral banks and the bilateral donors do not mobilise exactly the same coding system: the former reason in climate co-benefits under a common methodology of the multilateral banks, the latter use the OECD/DAC Rio markers. The common point is the declarative convention of purpose on which the climate qualification rests, rather than any identity of tools.

| Instrument | Amount | Price of capital | Possible way of counting | Controversy revealed |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| IBRD loan | €625M | About 3% | Near-full amount as mitigation climate finance | Concessionality issued from the Bank's AAA balance sheet, not from a budgetary subsidy |
| CTF loan | €32.8M / $38M | 0.25% | Face value, or a grant equivalent of about half the amount | Gap between money lent and real transfer |
| CTF grant | $2M | No repayment | Full amount | Simple case, but marginal in the total |
| Grid project | The whole operation | General infrastructure | "Principal" climate marker or mitigation co-benefit | Additionality and climate specificity debatable |

: One operation, several counting conventions {#tbl-turkiye}

The first controversy concerns concessionality. To say that a loan is concessional means that its terms are more favourable than the market's. But there are two ways to record it. At face value, the CTF loan counts for 38 million dollars. At grant equivalent, it counts only for the implicit subsidy contained in the loan, of the order of half the amount depending on the terms and the discount rate retained. The difference is not technical in the weak sense: it decides whether one is talking about a volume of financing or a net transfer.

The IBRD loan is more revealing still. It does not necessarily contain any budgetary subsidy from a donor. Its favour comes from the World Bank's capacity to borrow at low cost thanks to its balance sheet and its rating. If one compares the IBRD rate with the rate at which Türkiye would borrow alone, the advantage is real -- even if the comparison, between a loan in euros and a market cost denominated in dollars, remains indicative. But should it be counted as a climate contribution of the developed countries? Depending on the discount rate, the maturity retained and the rule applied, the same loan may cross the conventional concessionality threshold or fall short of it. What changes is not the financial flow; it is the measurement convention.

The second controversy concerns the climate qualification. A grid upgrade can allow the integration of renewables. It can therefore be declared as mitigation finance, either as a climate co-benefit in the methodology of the multilateral banks or as a climate objective in the vocabulary of the Rio markers. But a grid also carries the electricity produced by other sources; electrons have no colour. The climate qualification assigns a purpose to an infrastructure that serves the power system as a whole. It does not resolve the question of climate specificity. It closes it by convention.

The third controversy concerns additionality. The World Bank has been financing power grids for a long time. It is thus possible that the project would have existed, at least in part, without the climate finance category. Counting it as climate finance may record an additional resource; it may also relabel a development activity that was already likely. Here again, the convention does not merely describe reality: it carves it up.

This case justifies treating climate finance less as a category of money than as a counting procedure. Each step -- price of capital, grant equivalent, climate marker, attribution of purpose -- is governed by a convention. The post-Paris controversies do not spring from an absence of categories, then. They spring from the fact that the categories exist, yet remain open enough to sustain several incompatible readings.

### Four recurring controversies

The first controversy bears on the value of concessional loans. Donors have long had an interest in counting loans at face value: a loan of 100 million immediately raises the announced total by 100 million. Recipient countries and NGOs insist instead on the grant equivalent, because it alone measures the share actually transferred. The reform measuring ODA in grant equivalent, adopted by the DAC and implemented from 2018, shows that the question is central [@bachus_becault2017]. Oxfam's critical reports apply precisely this kind of correction to produce estimates far below the official figures [@carty_lecomte2018; @zagema2023]. This gap does not come from a calculation error: it translates two different definitions of effort.

The second controversy concerns the credibility of the Rio markers. The markers rest largely on donor self-declaration. Donors, though, have a direct incentive to qualify as climatic projects that would have been so partially, marginally, or not at all. The work of Axel and Katharina Michaelowa showed very early the risks of over-declaration and of inconsistency across donors [@michaelowa2007]. The problem goes beyond a few miscoded projects: the device turns a monitoring tool into an indicator of political compliance. From then on the marker no longer merely records an intention; it creates an interest in declaring that intention.

The third controversy bears on the attribution of mobilised private finance. If a public guarantee accompanies a private investment in a wind farm, what share of the private investment can be attributed to the guarantee? The whole difficulty comes from the counterfactual reasoning: what would have happened without public intervention? The methodologies developed by the OECD seek to standardise this attribution [@caruso_ellis2013; @jachnik2015], but they cannot eliminate judgement. Counting mobilised private finance broadly raises the totals and presents public finance as catalytic. Counting strictly lowers the amounts, but better protects the notion of a verifiable contribution [@stadelmann2013; @atteridge_dzebo2015].

The fourth controversy concerns the boundary between development aid and climate obligation. The principle of "new and additional" resources has been present since the Convention and was reaffirmed politically at Copenhagen. It never received a consensual operational definition. Additional to what? To existing ODA? To the target of 0.7% of GNI? To a counterfactual scenario of public spending? Several options are possible, and none prevails, because they redistribute the apparent effort differently [@stadelmann2011]. Roberts and Weikmans are right to read these controversies as distributive conflicts: they bear on the numbers, but the numbers bear on responsibility [@roberts_weikmans2017].

These four controversies carry over into the passage from the $100 billion goal to the new collective quantified goal. When the OECD announces that the 100 billion threshold was crossed in 2022, the conclusion depends on the conventions retained: markers, face value, instruments included, attribution of private finance.^[OECD (2024), *Climate Finance Provided and Mobilised by Developed Countries in 2013-2022*, OECD Publishing, Paris: the declared total for 2022 reaches $115.9 billion.] At Baku, the new goal of $300 billion by 2035 renews the same architecture, and widens it.^[Decision of the CMA at COP29 (Baku, November 2024) on the new collective quantified goal on climate finance, set at $300 billion per year by 2035.] The debates on the possible contribution of some emerging countries, on the treatment of South-South flows, on loss and damage or on the share of grants are not peripheral questions. They reopen the boundary of the object.

The post-Paris field is thus "established" in a particular sense. The categories exist. The institutions publish series. The reports answer one another. The numbers circulate. What stabilises, though, is a common language of dispute more than an agreement on substance. The same categories -- grant equivalent, marker, mobilisation, additionality -- let the actors cooperate enough to produce numbers and diverge enough to keep contesting them.

The computational analysis points in the same direction. After 2015 the themes diversify: green finance, green bonds, ESG, climate risks, loss and damage, just transition. But no break comparable to that of 2007--2009 appears in the categories of measurement. The Paris Agreement modifies the institutional architecture and strengthens the transparency obligations; it does not replace the accounting conventions inherited from the crystallization period. The efficiency/accountability polarisation grows sharper without breaking the field apart.

## Conclusion: ambiguity as a resource of government {#sec-conclusion}

The history retraced here overturns a common intuition. One might think that climate finance remains controversial because its categories are insufficiently defined. This is true in part, but insufficient. Climate finance became governable precisely because its categories are defined enough to allow declaration and comparison, and ambiguous enough to aggregate contradictory interests.

The 1992 starting point was relatively precise: developed countries were to cover agreed incremental costs. That frame had an economic and legal coherence. But it was narrow, costly to apply and politically binding. The later trajectory -- Kyoto mechanisms, dedicated funds, the Copenhagen commitment, Article 9 of Paris, the Baku goal -- progressively widens the object. It replaces the logic of reimbursing defined costs with that of mobilising a financial aggregate. This displacement makes it possible to count more things: loans, guarantees, multilateral financing, private co-financing, de-risking instruments. It offers a degree of diplomatic blur that holds two competing interpretations together: for the donors, an effort of mobilisation; for the recipients and the NGOs, an obligation of transfer and accountability.

Economists, practitioners as much as academics, occupy a constrained position in this history. They do not set the political goals. They do not decide the diplomatic compromises alone. But they supply the devices that make these compromises operative. By constructing the markers, the grant-equivalent methods, the rules for attributing private finance and the monitoring and reporting frameworks, they redefine the boundaries of the object. They perform boundary work [@gieryn1983]: between aid and climate, between loan and transfer, between mobilisation and obligation, between financial efficiency and distributive justice.

Climate finance thus offers a privileged observatory of the political limits of economic quantification. Quantification does not pacify the conflict; it makes it administrable. It transforms a dispute over historical responsibility, equity and development into a dispute over accounting categories. This displacement is productive: without it, collective commitments would be unverifiable; with it, they become verifiable only at the price of recurring controversies over what is being verified.

This conclusion opens several work sites. The first is archival: a finer history of the committees of the OECD, the UNFCCC and the multilateral banks would show how some conventions were retained rather than others. The second is geographic: the history of climate finance seen from the recipient countries, and not only from the reporting institutions dominated by the donors, remains largely unwritten. Climate finance is indeed governable because the conventions make the conflict measurable, but also because they displace the conflict towards arenas where technical capacities are unequally distributed. It is the donors who define and measure. The third is legal: the passage from the incremental costs of Article 4.3 to the collective quantified goal of Baku deserves an institutional history of its own. Finally, the financing of "loss and damage" is a decisive test of the viability of the agreements on climate finance. If this category is absorbed by the existing conventions, the strategic ambiguity of climate finance will be renewed. If it forces other instruments of measurement, it could reopen what Copenhagen had closed: the question of what is owed, to whom, and why.
